section.title: Written Assignments
section.p: Please see the for the homework due dates.
As the term progresses, more written assignments will be added to the list here. These should be turned in no later than their posted due dates. They can be turn in in-person, or uploaded in D2L.

Written assignments are collectively worth 20 of your final grade. You cannot expect to do well in this course unless you are turning these assignments in. These are where you will get to develop your mathematical writing skills and work on your ability to give a convincing logical argument.

The most important thing to understand about your written homework is that you are not merely finding and giving answers. You are explaining to someone what the problem was, how you handled it, and summarizing the results. In general, your write-ups should respect these four C's:
self-Containment
The response to the question that you submit should make it clear what the original question was. Put yourself in the shoes of another student from the class who does not have the original homework assignment in front of them. Would they be able to understand what you are talking about and what you are trying to accomplish? At a minimum, this means that you must give an introduction of some kind that lets your reader know what you are about to investigate. Also, include any and all charts, graphs, and mathematical expressions that were given in the problem, and explain their meaning, even if all you do is use labels.
Clarity
Am I able to read your work? Is your writing legible? Have you written complete sentences that make sense, where appropriate? If you have charts, graphs, and mathematical expressions, have you clearly indicated what they represent? Generally, any graphs need their axes labeled with regularly spaced tick marks, and they need some kind of overall title. If something about your write-up causes me to pause and wonder what you mean, you will lose clarity points.
Convincing
Even if your writing is clear, it might not be logically convicning. For example, I rode my bike to school today, so I only had $5 in my wallet. is clear in the sense that the sentence structure is fine. But I don't understand why riding your bike to school implies that you only have $5 in your wallet. The logical progression of your write-up should convince your reader that you know what you are talking about, and that each statement you write is correct based on other things you have already established in your write-up.
Conclusion
At the end of any write-up, there should be a clear, pleasant conclusion statement. Together with the introduction this allows a reader to read where your story started, and how it ended. And (only if they want to) it allows them to skip over reading the details of your story. (Maybe they will go back and read the details after they understand the overview.) If the exercise had context, the conclusion statement should use that content. For example, x=70 or He needs $70 are not good conclusion statements if something like Dmitri needs $70 in order to purchase a new lawnmower is better. For problems without context, conclusion statements like So the domain of f is [0,\infty). are acceptable.
Each exercise will be scored out of 4 points, one for each of these categories. A zero in some category doesn't really mean 0%, it just means I felt there was significant room for improvement and you coudl try again.

li.title: Written HW 1
li.p: Section 1.3 #22. Construct a 3\times3 matrix A, with nonzero entries, and a vector \vec{b} in \mathbb{R}^3 such that \vec{b} is not in the set spanned by the columns of A. (You should explain the thinking/process you use to make the matrix A. You should also explain the thinking/process you use to make the vector \vec{b}. And you should explain how we know that \vec{b} is not in the set spanned by the columns of A.)

li.title: Written HW 2
li.p: Section 1.4 #44. Suppose A is a 3\times3 matrix and \vec{b} is a vector in \mathbb{R}^3 with the property that A\vec{x}=\vec{b} has a unique solution. Explain why the columns of A must span \mathbb{R}^3.

li.title: Written HW 3
li.p: Section 1.5 #50. Suppose A is a 3\times3 matrix and \vec{y} is a vector in \mathbb{R}^3 such that the equation A\vec{x}=\vec{y} does not have a solution. Does there exist a vector z such that the equation A\vec{x}=\vec{z} has a unique solution? (That is a yes/no question, but you need to explain your answer to your reader in a way that convinces them.)

li.title: Written HW 4
li.p: Section 1.7 #44. Prove the following statement (which is true): If \vec{v}_1,\ldots,\vec{v}_4 are linearly independent vectors in \mathbb{R}^4, then \{\vec{v}_1,\vec{v}_2,\vec{v}_3\} is also linearly independent. (This exercise asks you to demonstrate you can work with the official definition of linear independence. If you are not writing an explanation that involves a sum of scaled vectors equalling zero, then you are not explaining this well.)

li.title: Written HW 5
li.p: Section 1.8 #44. Let T:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^3 be the transformation that projects each vector \vec{x}=(x_1,x_2,x_3) onto the plane x_2=0, so T(x_1,x_2,x_3)=(x_1,0,x_3). Show that T is a linear transformation. (This exercise asks you to demonstrate familiarity with the official definition of a linear transformation.)

li.title: Written HW 6
li.p: Section 2.2 #32. Explain why the columns of an n\times n matrix A span \mathbb{R}^n when A is invertible. Hint: Review Theorem 4 from Section 1.4. (There's more than one way to do this, but I recommend you investigate item (a) from Theorem 4, and then conclude about item (c). The only thing you have to work with is that A is invertible. So some matrix A^{-1} exists, and AA^{-1}=I. What happens if you take that equation and right-multiply each side by some vector \vec{x}?)

li.title: Written HW 7
li.p: Section 2.3 #30. Assume H is an n\times n matrix. If the equation H\vec{x}=\vec{c} is inconsistent for some \vec{c} in \mathbb{R}^n, what can you say about the equation H\vec{x}=\vec{0}? Why? (Hint: Study the Invertible Matrix Theorem from the start of section 2.3 well. What does it say about H?)

li.title: Written HW 8
li.p: Section 3.2 #36. Use Therorem 3 from Section 3.2 (but not Theorem 4) to show that if two rows of a square matrix are equal, then \det A=0.

li.title: Written HW 9
li.p: Section 4.2 #42. Let T:V\to W be a linear transformation from a vector space V into a vector space W. Prove that the range of T is a subspace of W. (Hint: Typical elements of the range have the form T(\vec{x}) and T(\vec{w} for some \vec{x} and \vec{w} in V.) [This exercise is asking you to demonstrate that you understand a few things. Among them: what is the meaning of linear transformation and what is the meaning of subspace.]

li.title: Written HW 10
li.p: Section 4.5 #31. Let S be a subset of an n-dimensional vector space V, and suppose S contains fewer than n vectors. Explain why S cannot span V [This exercise is asking you to demonstrate that you understand a few things. Among them: since V is n-dimensional, then there exists a what? And how many things are in that what? And then how can you use these things to represent the vectors from S? How large are these things representing the vectors from S and how many of these representations are there? Somewhere in all this, there is a matrix, and how many pivot positions does it have? What does Theorem 4 from Section 1.4 say about that?]
